title: GDPR Articles 5, 13, 17 — Data Processing Principles, Transparency, and Erasure
content:
Article 5 — Principles Relating to Processing of Personal Data: Personal data shall be (a) processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (lawfulness, fairness and transparency); (b) collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner incompatible with those purposes (purpose limitation); (c) adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (data minimisation); (d) accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date — every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that are inaccurate are erased or rectified without delay (accuracy); (e) kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed (storage limitation); (f) processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (integrity and confidentiality). The controller shall be responsible for, and be able to demonstrate compliance with, paragraph 1 (accountability).

Article 13 — Information to Be Provided Where Personal Data Are Collected from the Data Subject: At the time personal data are collected, the controller shall provide the data subject with the following: the identity and contact details of the controller; contact details of the data protection officer where applicable; the purposes and the legal basis for the processing; where processing is based on legitimate interests, those interests; any recipients or categories of recipients; where applicable, the fact that the controller intends to transfer personal data to a third country. In addition, the controller shall provide: the period for which personal data will be stored, or the criteria used to determine that period; the existence of the rights to request access, rectification, erasure, restriction of processing, data portability, and to object to processing; where processing is based on consent, the right to withdraw consent at any time; the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority; whether provision of personal data is a statutory or contractual requirement, or a requirement necessary to enter into a contract, and the possible consequences of failing to provide such data; the existence of automated decision-making, including profiling, and meaningful information about the logic involved.

Article 17 — Right to Erasure ('Right to Be Forgotten'): The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning them without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: (a) the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; (b) the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based and there is no other legal ground for the processing; (c) the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21(1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing; (d) the personal data have been unlawfully processed; (e) the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law; (f) the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services to a child. The right to erasure does not apply where processing is necessary for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation; for reasons of public interest in the area of public health; for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes, or statistical purposes; or for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims.

title: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.8 — Technological Controls
content:
Annex A.8 of ISO/IEC 27001:2022 defines 34 technological controls that organisations implementing an Information Security Management System (ISMS) must consider. Key controls are summarised below.

A.8.2 Privileged Access Rights: The allocation and use of privileged access rights shall be restricted, controlled, and regularly reviewed. Privileged accounts must be provisioned separately from standard user accounts, subject to multi-factor authentication, and reviewed at minimum quarterly. Just-in-time (JIT) privileged access is the recommended implementation pattern.

A.8.3 Information Access Restriction: Access to information and application system functions shall be restricted in accordance with the access control policy. Role-based access control (RBAC) aligned to the principle of least privilege is the standard implementation. Access rights must be reviewed when roles change and revoked promptly upon departure.

A.8.5 Secure Authentication: Secure authentication technologies and procedures shall be implemented based on information access restrictions. Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is required for all privileged access, remote access, and access to sensitive data. Password policies must enforce minimum length, complexity, and prohibit reuse of recent passwords.

A.8.8 Management of Technical Vulnerabilities: Patch management timelines must be defined by severity: critical vulnerabilities within 48 hours, high within 14 days, medium within 30 days. Vulnerability scans must be performed at minimum monthly on internet-facing systems.

A.8.15 Logging: Logs that record user activities, exceptions, faults, and other relevant security events shall be produced, stored, protected, and analysed. Log retention must meet applicable regulatory requirements (typically 12–24 months online). Logs must be protected against tampering and reviewed through automated alerting or SIEM tooling.

A.8.24 Use of Cryptography: Minimum standards — AES-256 for data at rest, TLS 1.2 or higher for data in transit, RSA-2048 or ECC-256 for asymmetric operations. Cryptographic keys must be rotated on a defined schedule and stored in dedicated key management infrastructure.

A.8.25 Secure Development Life Cycle: Security requirements must be defined at the requirements phase, threat modelling performed at design, and security testing integrated into the CI/CD pipeline.

A.8.28 Secure Coding: OWASP Top 10 compliance is the minimum baseline. Static application security testing (SAST) tools must be integrated into build pipelines. Developers must receive annual secure coding training.

title: Basel III — Capital Adequacy, Liquidity Standards, and Leverage Requirements
source: Basel Committee on Banking Supervision — Basel III: A Global Regulatory Framework (June 2011, revised 2019)
content:
Basel III introduces strengthened global standards for bank capital adequacy, stress testing, and liquidity risk. Key requirements are summarised below.

Minimum Capital Requirements: Banks must hold a minimum Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio of 4.5% of risk-weighted assets (RWA). Tier 1 capital (CET1 plus Additional Tier 1) must be at least 6% of RWA. Total capital (Tier 1 plus Tier 2) must be at least 8% of RWA. CET1 consists primarily of common shares, retained earnings, and other comprehensive income, subject to regulatory adjustments including deductions for goodwill, deferred tax assets, and significant investments in financial institutions.

Capital Conservation Buffer: Banks must hold an additional Capital Conservation Buffer of 2.5% of RWA composed entirely of CET1 capital. This buffer is designed to ensure banks build up capital outside periods of stress. Banks that draw on the buffer face restrictions on dividends, share buybacks, and discretionary bonus payments. The effective minimum CET1 ratio including the conservation buffer is 7%.

Countercyclical Capital Buffer: National supervisors may impose a Countercyclical Capital Buffer of 0% to 2.5% of RWA in periods of excess credit growth. The buffer is released in downturns to support continued lending. Canadian institutions are subject to OSFI's determination of the domestic countercyclical buffer rate, which is published quarterly.

Systemically Important Banks (G-SIBs and D-SIBs): Global systemically important banks (G-SIBs) must hold an additional loss absorbency surcharge of 1% to 3.5% of RWA based on their systemic importance score. Domestic systemically important banks (D-SIBs), designated by national supervisors, face a surcharge of up to 2%.

Leverage Ratio: A non-risk-based leverage ratio backstop requires Tier 1 capital of at least 3% of total exposure (on-balance-sheet assets plus off-balance-sheet exposures). For G-SIBs, an additional leverage ratio buffer of 50% of the G-SIB surcharge applies.

Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR): Banks must hold sufficient High-Quality Liquid Assets (HQLA) to cover total net cash outflows over a 30-day stress period, resulting in an LCR of at least 100%. HQLA are divided into Level 1 assets (cash, central bank reserves, certain sovereign debt — no haircut) and Level 2 assets (certain corporate and covered bonds — subject to 15%–50% haircuts and a 40% cap on total HQLA).

Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR): The NSFR requires Available Stable Funding (ASF) to be at least 100% of Required Stable Funding (RSF) over a one-year horizon. ASF includes equity, long-term liabilities, and stable deposits. RSF reflects the liquidity characteristics of assets and off-balance-sheet commitments. The NSFR is designed to reduce funding risk by encouraging banks to fund long-term assets with stable liabilities.

Interest Rate Risk in the Banking Book (IRRBB): Banks must measure and manage interest rate risk in the banking book. Supervisory shock scenarios include parallel shifts, steepeners, flatteners, and short-rate shocks. A bank whose Economic Value of Equity (EVE) declines by more than 15% of Tier 1 capital under any standardised shock scenario is considered an outlier institution and subject to supervisory review.

Operational Risk: Under the Standardised Approach for Operational Risk (SA), the capital requirement is calculated as a function of the Business Indicator (a proxy for bank size) and an Internal Loss Multiplier (based on historical operational losses). Banks with a Business Indicator above EUR 1 billion must incorporate internal loss data into the calculation.

Disclosure and Pillar 3: Banks must publicly disclose capital ratios, RWA composition, leverage ratio, LCR, and NSFR on a quarterly basis. Disclosures must follow the standardised templates prescribed by the Basel Committee to ensure comparability across institutions and jurisdictions.

title: OSFI Guideline E-23 — Model Risk Management (2025 Final, Effective May 1, 2027)
source: Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI), Guideline E-23 — Model Risk Management, September 2025 (final), effective May 1, 2027
content:
OSFI Guideline E-23 (Model Risk Management) was published in final form in September 2025 and becomes effective May 1, 2027 after an 18-month transition period. It applies to all federally regulated financial institutions (FRFIs) — including banks, foreign bank branches, life and property-and-casualty insurers, and trust and loan companies — but does not apply to federally regulated pension plans.

OSFI Guideline E-23 (2025 final) establishes updated expectations for federally regulated financial institutions (FRFIs) on model risk management, reflecting the rapid expansion of artificial intelligence and machine learning in financial services and lessons from OSFI supervisory experience since the 2017 edition.

Expanded Definition of a Model: The 2024 revision broadens the model definition to explicitly include AI/ML systems, large language models (LLMs), and generative AI tools used in material business decisions. A model is any quantitative or algorithmic system — including rule-based systems, statistical models, machine learning models, and foundation models accessed via API — that processes inputs to produce outputs used in risk measurement, customer decisions, financial reporting, or regulatory capital calculations. The three-component structure (inputs, processing, reporting) is retained, and the standard that spreadsheets used for material decisions constitute models is reaffirmed.

Model Risk Definition and Expanded Sources: Model risk is the potential for adverse financial, reputational, or regulatory consequences from decisions based on incorrect, biased, or misused model outputs. The 2024 revision adds two sources to the original two: (3) data risk — model outputs degraded by poor-quality, unrepresentative, or biased training or input data; and (4) deployment risk — model behaviour in production that differs materially from validated test conditions, including due to distributional shift or prompt injection in LLM contexts.

Three Lines of Defence: The 2024 revision makes the three lines of defence structure explicit. First line (model owners and users) — responsible for model development, documentation, day-to-day monitoring, and escalating issues. Second line (independent model risk function) — responsible for setting MRM policy, maintaining the model inventory, conducting or overseeing independent validation, and challenging first-line risk assessments. Third line (internal audit) — provides independent assurance over the design and operating effectiveness of the MRM framework. Each line must have clearly defined mandates, sufficient expertise, and access to the model inventory and validation documentation.

Model Governance Framework: The MRM framework must include: (a) a Board-approved model risk policy and risk appetite statement; (b) a complete, centrally governed model inventory; (c) documented standards for development, validation, approval, monitoring, and retirement; (d) clearly defined roles for model owners, developers, validators, and users; (e) escalation procedures for material findings, limitations, and overrides; (f) a dedicated second-line model risk function with sufficient seniority and independence. The framework must be proportionate to the FRFI's size, complexity, and model portfolio but must cover all models meeting the materiality threshold regardless of model type or technology.

Model Development Standards: Model development documentation must cover: purpose, scope, and use restrictions; theoretical or algorithmic basis; data sources, lineage, and quality assessment; in-sample and out-of-sample testing results; benchmarking against alternative approaches; identified limitations and conditions of failure; and, for AI/ML models, training methodology, hyperparameter choices, and feature importance. Models developed using third-party frameworks or foundation models must document reliance on external components and the FRFI's independent testing scope.

Model Validation: Independent validation is mandatory before deployment in any material application and at minimum annually thereafter for high-risk models. Validators must be organisationally independent from model developers and have no stake in model outcomes. Validation scope must include: conceptual soundness; data quality; outcome analysis; sensitivity and stability testing; benchmarking; and, for AI/ML models, fairness assessment and explainability review. Validation findings must be rated by severity, tracked to remediation, and reported to senior management and the Board.

Generative AI and Large Language Models: The 2024 revision introduces specific expectations for LLMs and generative AI tools used in material applications. FRFIs must: register all such tools in the model inventory; define and document acceptable use boundaries (use cases, user populations, output review requirements); assess and mitigate hallucination risk — outputs that are fluent but factually incorrect; implement human review requirements proportionate to the materiality and reversibility of decisions influenced by LLM outputs; and monitor for prompt injection and adversarial manipulation. LLMs used for customer-facing communications, compliance advice, or credit decisions require the most stringent governance.

Ongoing Monitoring and Model Retirement: Performance monitoring KPIs must be defined at approval and tracked continuously. Material performance degradation triggers revalidation. Models must be formally retired through a documented process; documentation retained seven years post-retirement. The model inventory must be reconciled to production systems quarterly to identify unregistered or shadow models.

title: OSFI Guideline B-20 — Residential Mortgage Underwriting Practices and Procedures
source: Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI), Guideline B-20, October 2017
content:
OSFI Guideline B-20 sets out OSFI's expectations for prudent residential mortgage underwriting at federally regulated financial institutions (FRFIs). The guideline applies to all FRFIs that originate residential mortgage loans in Canada.

Mortgage Stress Test — Minimum Qualifying Rate: FRFIs must qualify all uninsured residential mortgage borrowers at the greater of: (a) the Bank of Canada's five-year benchmark rate; or (b) the contractual mortgage rate plus 200 basis points. This stress test is designed to ensure borrowers can service their debt obligations under a rising interest rate environment. For insured mortgages, the applicable minimum qualifying rate is set by the federal government through the mortgage insurance rules.

Loan-to-Value (LTV) Limits: FRFIs must establish and adhere to maximum LTV ratios for all residential mortgage products. For uninsured mortgages, the maximum LTV is 80% at origination. FRFIs must apply appropriate LTV limits to refinancing, second mortgages, and HELOCs (home equity lines of credit). LTV must be calculated using the lower of the purchase price or appraised value. FRFIs must use qualified, independent appraisers and maintain appraisal standards that are consistent with industry best practices.

Debt Service Coverage: FRFIs must assess each borrower's ability to service their total debt obligations. The Gross Debt Service (GDS) ratio — housing costs as a percentage of gross income — must generally not exceed 39%. The Total Debt Service (TDS) ratio — all debt obligations as a percentage of gross income — must generally not exceed 44%. FRFIs must use the stressed qualifying rate (not the contract rate) when calculating GDS and TDS ratios for the stress test.

Borrower Identity and Income Verification: FRFIs must verify the identity of all borrowers and co-borrowers in accordance with applicable anti-money laundering (AML) and know-your-customer (KYC) requirements. Income used to qualify borrowers must be verified through reliable, third-party documentation. Self-employed borrower income must be documented through two years of Notice of Assessment from the Canada Revenue Agency or equivalent. Stated income programs without verification are prohibited for FRFIs.

Property Valuation: FRFIs must implement a property valuation process that ensures the appraised value reflects the market value of the property at the time of origination. Automated valuation models (AVMs) may be used for low-risk transactions subject to validation against physical appraisals. FRFIs must maintain policies that govern acceptable collateral types, geographic concentration limits, and property condition requirements.

Non-Conforming and Exception Lending: FRFIs must establish clear policies for exceptions to standard underwriting criteria. Exceptions must be approved by an authority level commensurate with the risk, documented with a written rationale, and tracked in a central exception register. Exception rates must be monitored and reported to senior management. Systemic exception patterns must be investigated and addressed through policy review.

Portfolio Management and Stress Testing: FRFIs must conduct regular stress testing of their residential mortgage portfolios under adverse scenarios including significant house price declines (minimum 25%), interest rate increases, and unemployment shocks. Stress test results must be reported to senior management and the Board and incorporated into capital and liquidity planning. Geographic concentration risk must be monitored and managed within approved limits.

Condominium and High-Rise Lending: FRFIs must apply additional due diligence to condominium lending given the concentration risks associated with single-project exposure. Policies must address presale financing, project completion risk, and the financial health of the condominium corporation. Exposure to a single project or developer must be subject to aggregate limits.

Governance and Oversight: The Board of Directors must approve the residential mortgage underwriting policy and receive regular reporting on portfolio performance, exception rates, and stress test results. Senior management is responsible for implementing the policy and maintaining appropriate controls. Internal audit must independently assess compliance with the guideline on a risk-based schedule.

title: OSFI E-23 — Third-Party and Vendor Model Risk Management (2024)
source: Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI), Guideline E-23 — Model Risk Management, September 2025 (final), effective May 1, 2027
content:
OSFI Guideline E-23 (2024) applies to models regardless of whether they are developed internally or sourced from a third party. The 2024 revision significantly strengthens expectations for third-party and vendor model risk, reflecting the widespread adoption of cloud-hosted AI platforms, API-based foundation models, and vendor-supplied analytics in financial services.

Expanded Scope of Third-Party Models: Third-party models now explicitly include: vendor-supplied credit scoring and fraud detection models; externally hosted ML platforms accessed via API; foundation models (including LLMs) provided by technology companies under commercial agreements; open-source models deployed by the FRFI; and models embedded in enterprise software (e.g., treasury management, ALM, and risk systems). The FRFI's accountability for model risk is unchanged regardless of the delivery mechanism — cloud-hosted models carry the same governance obligations as on-premise deployments.

Pre-Deployment Due Diligence: Before deploying any third-party model in a material application, FRFIs must: review vendor documentation on methodology, training data, intended use, and known limitations; assess the vendor's AI/ML development practices, including data governance, bias testing, and model change management; obtain sufficient information to conduct independent validation — including sample inputs and outputs, methodology documentation, and (where possible) access to model weights or algorithmic specifications; evaluate the vendor's financial stability and continuity arrangements; and, for foundation models, assess the vendor's acceptable use policies and the FRFI's obligations under those policies.

Validation of Third-Party and Foundation Models: Validation of third-party models must follow the same standards as internally developed models. For foundation models and LLMs, validation must additionally assess: task-specific performance on the FRFI's actual use cases using the FRFI's own data; hallucination rate and factual accuracy in the relevant domain; sensitivity to prompt variation (prompt robustness testing); and fairness across protected demographic groups where the model influences customer-facing decisions. Where the vendor restricts access to model internals, FRFIs must compensate through enhanced behavioural testing, challenger modelling, and outcome monitoring.

Contractual Protections — Updated Requirements: Vendor contracts for material models must include: advance notification of model updates or retraining (minimum 30 days for significant changes); continued access to documentation and data sufficient for ongoing monitoring; the right to audit or commission independent review of the vendor's model governance practices; data residency, privacy, and security obligations consistent with OSFI's Technology and Cyber Risk guidelines; liability provisions addressing model errors that cause financial harm; and transition assistance obligations. Contracts for cloud-hosted AI services must address regulatory examination access — OSFI must be able to examine relevant model documentation regardless of the hosting arrangement.

Concentration Risk in Third-Party Models: The 2024 revision introduces explicit expectations around concentration risk. FRFIs that rely on a single vendor for multiple material models, or that use the same foundation model across multiple risk domains, must assess and document the correlated failure risk. Concentration limits must be established in the model risk appetite, and contingency plans developed for scenarios where a vendor model becomes unavailable or materially degraded.

Ongoing Monitoring: Vendor model updates must trigger a review proportionate to the materiality of changes. FRFIs must maintain a registry of vendor advisories and limitation disclosures. Where a vendor ceases support for a model version in production use, a remediation plan approved by senior management is required within 90 days.

title: OSFI E-23 — Model Risk Appetite, Risk Rating, and Model Inventory (2024)
source: Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI), Guideline E-23 — Model Risk Management, September 2025 (final), effective May 1, 2027
content:
OSFI Guideline E-23 (2024) requires FRFIs to establish a structured, Board-approved framework for classifying and managing model risk that is fully integrated with the institution's enterprise risk appetite.

Model Risk Appetite — Enhanced Requirements: The Board must approve a model risk appetite statement that is operationally specific, not aspirational. The 2024 revision requires the appetite statement to address: (a) maximum tolerable exposure in any single model or correlated model cluster; (b) tolerance levels for models operating under known limitations, differentiated by model risk tier; (c) maximum acceptable proportion of the model portfolio without a current-cycle validation; (d) concentration limits on third-party and foundation model dependencies; (e) thresholds for Board escalation versus CRO escalation; and (f) specific treatment of AI/ML and generative AI model risk given their distinct failure modes. The risk appetite must be reviewed annually and following any material expansion of the model portfolio or adoption of a new model technology.

Model Risk Rating Framework: Each model must be assigned a risk rating using a consistent, documented methodology. Rating factors include: (a) materiality — financial, capital, or reputational impact of model error, assessed under both normal and stressed conditions; (b) complexity and opacity — the degree to which outputs can be independently verified, with AI/ML models generally rated higher due to reduced interpretability; (c) use context — regulatory capital and financial reporting models rated most stringently; customer-facing and credit decision models rated high; internal MIS models rated based on downstream materiality; (d) data dependency — models relying on sparse, proprietary, or potentially biased datasets carry elevated data risk; (e) validation currency — models not validated within their required cycle are automatically escalated one rating tier. The 2024 revision adds an AI/ML complexity premium: models using deep learning, transformer architectures, or reinforcement learning must be rated at minimum medium risk.

Model Inventory — Updated Mandatory Fields: The model inventory must now include, in addition to the 2017 fields: model technology type (statistical, ML, LLM, rule-based, hybrid); data lineage summary; third-party dependency flag and vendor name; AI/ML-specific fields (training data vintage, retraining schedule, fairness assessment status); regulatory capital use flag; climate risk application flag; and human review requirement (automated decision, human-in-the-loop, human-on-the-loop). The inventory must be reconciled to production systems quarterly; any model in production use not registered in the inventory is a Significant finding.

High-Risk Model Oversight: High-risk models require: annual independent validation minimum; quarterly KPI reporting to the CRO; annual benchmarking against a challenger approach; and Board-level reporting for unresolved Significant findings. Regulatory capital models with open Significant findings may not be used for capital calculation without CRO sign-off and a Board-approved remediation plan with milestones.

Model Override Governance: Overrides must be documented, named, justified, tracked, and reviewed quarterly. Sustained directional override patterns trigger mandatory revalidation. The 2024 revision extends override governance to AI/ML systems: human review decisions that systematically reject or adjust model outputs in the same direction must be tracked as effective overrides and reviewed for model performance signals.

title: OSFI E-23 — OSFI Supervisory Review and Examination Expectations (2024)
source: Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI), Guideline E-23 — Model Risk Management, September 2025 (final), effective May 1, 2027
content:
OSFI uses Guideline E-23 (2024) as the primary reference when assessing model risk management practices during supervisory reviews. The 2024 revision introduces more structured examination criteria and explicit supervisory expectations for AI/ML governance.

Supervisory Assessment Approach: OSFI reviews MRM as part of a Supervisory Risk Assessment (SRA) or a targeted MRM examination. The 2024 revision formalises a tiered examination intensity: Tier 1 institutions (D-SIBs) face annual targeted MRM reviews; other FRFIs are reviewed on a risk-based schedule. OSFI may commission independent third-party model reviews where material supervisory concerns are identified. Examination findings are communicated through Management Letters and tracked to resolution.

Key Areas of Examiner Focus (2024 Updates): Examiners assess: (a) model inventory completeness — OSFI reconciles the inventory against production systems and flags unregistered models as Significant; (b) AI/ML governance — specifically whether the FRFI has extended E-23 controls to LLMs and generative AI, including hallucination controls, human review requirements, and prompt injection risk management; (c) independence and expertise of the validation function — validators must have demonstrable expertise in the model types they validate, including AI/ML expertise for institutions with material AI exposure; (d) third-party model oversight — whether vendor contracts meet 2024 requirements and whether foundation model dependencies are subject to concentration risk assessment; (e) Board and CRO oversight quality — OSFI expects MRM reporting to include AI/ML-specific risk indicators and climate model status; (f) remediation management — open Significant findings from prior examinations with missed milestones are the highest-priority concern.

Supervisory Findings Classification: (1) Significant — immediate management attention required, committed remediation plan with Board awareness; (2) Moderate — remediation within next supervisory cycle; (3) Housekeeping — minor improvement. Repeated Significant findings in the same domain trigger escalated action: capital add-ons, restricted model use, mandatory external review, or Supervisory Requirement. For AI/ML models, a first-time Significant finding on explainability or hallucination risk may warrant immediate use restrictions on customer-facing applications.

Regulatory Capital Model Approval: Internal model approvals (IRB, IMA) require pre-application engagement with OSFI, full documentation and validation submission, OSFI on-site testing, and ongoing conditions. The 2024 revision adds a requirement that FRFIs notify OSFI within 30 days of any material change to an approved model, including retraining of AI/ML models used in capital calculations. OSFI may withdraw approval following unresolved Significant findings or unreported material changes.

AI/ML Supervisory Focus Areas for 2024-2026: OSFI has signalled that AI/ML model governance, generative AI in customer-facing and compliance applications, and climate risk model integration are priority examination areas for the 2024-2026 supervisory cycle. FRFIs should expect targeted questions on LLM governance frameworks, hallucination controls, and human oversight mechanisms during upcoming examinations.

title: OSFI E-23 — ICAAP Models and Stress Testing Governance (2024)
source: Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI), Guideline E-23 — Model Risk Management, September 2025 (final), effective May 1, 2027
content:
Models used in the Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process (ICAAP) and enterprise stress testing are subject to the full requirements of OSFI Guideline E-23, with additional expectations reflecting the material role these models play in capital planning and regulatory reporting.

ICAAP Model Scope: ICAAP models include models used to estimate credit risk losses under adverse scenarios, market risk VaR and stressed VaR models, operational risk scenario models, net interest income (NII) and economic value of equity (EVE) models for IRRBB, and any quantitative tool used to translate a macroeconomic scenario into projected capital requirements. All such models must be registered in the model inventory and subject to independent validation on the schedule appropriate to their risk rating.

Scenario Design and Model Integration: FRFIs must document how macroeconomic scenarios are translated into model inputs, including the assumptions and judgements applied at each stage of the scenario-to-projection chain. Where multiple models are linked in a sequence (e.g., a macro model feeding a credit loss model feeding a capital ratio projection), the interaction effects and error propagation across models must be assessed and documented. Validators must review both individual model performance and the integrated scenario projection chain.

Sensitivity Analysis Requirements: ICAAP stress test models must be subject to comprehensive sensitivity analysis that tests: (a) the impact of changes in key model parameters and assumptions; (b) the sensitivity of capital projections to scenario severity; (c) the effect of model uncertainty on the range of possible capital outcomes. Sensitivity analysis results must be presented to the Board alongside point estimates to convey model uncertainty and avoid false precision in capital adequacy assessments.

Reverse Stress Testing: FRFIs must use ICAAP models to conduct reverse stress testing — identifying the combination of scenarios and model outcomes that would cause the institution to breach its regulatory capital minimums or fail. Reverse stress test results must be reviewed by the Board annually and used to inform risk appetite setting and contingency planning. Model limitations that prevent meaningful reverse stress testing must be identified and remediated.

Documentation and Auditability: All ICAAP model runs must be fully documented and reproducible. Model inputs, parameter assumptions, scenario specifications, and output files must be archived with sufficient detail to allow an independent party to replicate the results. Version control must be applied to all models and data used in a submitted ICAAP. OSFI may request model run replication as part of its supervisory review of the ICAAP submission.

title: OSFI E-23 — Climate Risk Models and Emerging Model Risk (2024)
source: Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI), Guideline E-23 — Model Risk Management, September 2025 (final), effective May 1, 2027; read with OSFI Guideline B-15 — Climate Risk Management (2023)
content:
OSFI expects FRFIs to apply the model risk management framework established under Guideline E-23 to climate-related financial risk models, recognising the significant uncertainty and methodological immaturity inherent in this emerging field.

Climate Risk Model Types: FRFIs may use climate models for: physical risk assessment — estimating losses from acute events (floods, wildfires, storms) and chronic climate shifts (sea level rise, temperature changes) on loan collateral, insurance exposures, and business continuity; transition risk assessment — modelling the financial impact of carbon pricing, regulatory change, and technology shifts on counterparty creditworthiness and asset values; climate scenario analysis — translating NGFS or OSFI-prescribed climate scenarios into financial impact projections for use in ICAAP and ORSA. Each of these model types must be registered in the model inventory and governed under E-23.

Heightened Uncertainty and Model Limitations: Climate models exhibit uncertainty that exceeds that of most traditional financial models, including: deep uncertainty about physical climate trajectories beyond 10-year horizons; limited historical data on climate-related financial losses; significant model-to-model variance in transition cost estimates; and the interaction of climate risk with existing credit, market, and operational risk models. FRFIs must explicitly document and disclose these limitations in model validation reports and in ICAAP/ORSA climate scenario disclosures. OSFI does not expect false precision — qualitative judgement-based overlays to model outputs are acceptable where quantitative approaches are immature.

Data Governance for Climate Models: Climate risk models rely on geospatial data, counterparty emissions data, and third-party climate projections. FRFIs must assess the quality, coverage, and vintage of all climate data inputs, apply E-23 data quality standards, and document data gaps and the judgements made to address them. Where counterparty emissions data is estimated (e.g., using sector proxies), the estimation methodology must be documented and validated.

Validation Standards for Climate Models: Given the limited outcome data available for back-testing, climate model validation must place greater weight on: conceptual soundness review — assessing the scientific basis of the physical or transition risk methodology; expert review — engaging independent climate science or climate economics expertise where internal capacity is limited; sensitivity analysis — assessing how outputs change under different data inputs and scenario assumptions; scenario coverage — ensuring the model produces plausible outputs across a range of climate scenarios including those prescribed by OSFI. Validators must clearly state the extent to which standard outcome analysis could and could not be performed and why.

Integration with Broader Risk Frameworks: Climate risk model outputs must be integrated into credit risk, market risk, and liquidity risk models where material. FRFIs must assess and document climate-related model interaction effects — for example, how physical risk to collateral values flows into LTV calculations and credit loss models. OSFI expects this integration to mature over successive ICAAP and ORSA cycles as data and methodologies improve.

title: PIPEDA — Key Principles for Personal Information Protection
source: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA), S.C. 2000, c. 5 — Schedule 1, Clauses 4.1–4.9
content:
PIPEDA establishes ten fair information principles that govern the collection, use, and disclosure of personal information in the course of commercial activities by private-sector organisations in Canada. The principles are set out in Schedule 1 of the Act.

Principle 1 — Accountability: An organisation is responsible for personal information under its control and shall designate an individual or individuals accountable for the organisation's compliance with the principles. The designated individual is commonly referred to as the Chief Privacy Officer or Privacy Officer. Accountability extends to personal information transferred to third parties for processing — the organisation must use contractual or other means to ensure a comparable level of protection.

Principle 2 — Identifying Purposes: The purposes for which personal information is collected shall be identified by the organisation at or before the time the information is collected. Purposes must be documented and communicated to the individual in a manner that is clear and understandable. New uses of existing personal information require fresh consent unless a statutory exception applies.

Principle 3 — Consent: The knowledge and consent of the individual are required for the collection, use, or disclosure of personal information, except where inappropriate. Consent may be express or implied depending on the sensitivity of the information and the reasonable expectations of the individual. Consent must be meaningful — organisations may not use deceptive practices to obtain consent, and consent obtained under conditions of duress or deception is invalid. Individuals must be able to withdraw consent subject to legal or contractual restrictions.

Principle 4 — Limiting Collection: The collection of personal information shall be limited to that which is necessary for the purposes identified by the organisation. Information shall be collected by fair and lawful means. Organisations shall not collect personal information indiscriminately or by misleading individuals about the purpose of collection.

Principle 5 — Limiting Use, Disclosure, and Retention: Personal information shall not be used or disclosed for purposes other than those for which it was collected, except with the consent of the individual or as required by law. Personal information shall be retained only as long as necessary for the fulfilment of those purposes. Organisations must develop guidelines and implement procedures with respect to the retention of personal information and must destroy, erase, or make anonymous information that is no longer required.

Principle 6 — Accuracy: Personal information shall be as accurate, complete, and up-to-date as is necessary for the purposes for which it is to be used. Organisations must not use personal information to make a decision about an individual unless it is accurate and up-to-date. Individuals have the right to challenge the accuracy and completeness of their personal information.

Principle 7 — Safeguards: Personal information shall be protected by security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. The safeguards must protect personal information against loss or theft, as well as unauthorised access, disclosure, copying, use, or modification. Organisations must make their employees aware of the importance of maintaining the confidentiality of personal information. The nature of the safeguards will vary with the sensitivity, amount, distribution, and format of the information.

Principle 8 — Openness: An organisation shall make readily available to individuals specific information about its policies and practices relating to the management of personal information. This information must include: the name and title of the person accountable for the organisation's policies and practices; the means of gaining access to personal information held by the organisation; a description of the type of personal information held, including a general account of its use; and a copy of any brochures or other information that explain the organisation's policies, standards, or codes.

Principle 9 — Individual Access: Upon request, an individual shall be informed of the existence, use, and disclosure of his or her personal information and shall be given access to that information. An individual shall be able to challenge the accuracy and completeness of the information and have it amended as appropriate. Organisations must respond to access requests within 30 days. Requests may be denied where disclosure would reveal personal information about a third party, would reveal confidential commercial information, or is prohibited by law.

Principle 10 — Challenging Compliance: An individual shall be able to address a challenge concerning compliance with the above principles to the designated individual or individuals accountable for the organisation's compliance. Unresolved complaints may be referred to the Privacy Commissioner of Canada. The Commissioner may investigate complaints, conduct audits, and make recommendations. Courts may order organisations to correct practices and award damages to complainants.

title: NIST AI Risk Management Framework — Core Functions and Trustworthy AI Characteristics
source: NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0), National Institute of Standards and Technology, January 2023
content:
The NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF) provides a voluntary, non-prescriptive approach for organisations to manage risks associated with AI systems across their full lifecycle. It is organised around four core functions: Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage.

Govern is a cross-cutting function that establishes the organisational culture, policies, accountability structures, and processes needed to manage AI risk. It underpins all other functions. Govern activities include setting risk tolerance, defining roles and responsibilities for AI risk management, establishing processes for stakeholder engagement, and embedding risk management into the organisation's culture and incentive structures. Governance must be proportionate to the organisation's AI portfolio and the risks its systems present.

Map establishes context and identifies risks. Map activities include categorising AI systems by intended use, deployment context, and potential impact; identifying relevant stakeholders and potential harms; and surfacing risks related to bias, safety, security, privacy, and explainability before deployment. Map requires organisations to understand the sociotechnical context of their AI systems, not just the technical performance characteristics.

Measure analyses and tracks identified risks using quantitative and qualitative methods. Measure activities include evaluating AI system performance, testing for bias and fairness, assessing robustness and security, and monitoring outputs for accuracy and reliability. The NIST AI RMF emphasises that measurement methods should be appropriate to the system's risk level and intended use, and that metrics should be defined in advance of deployment.

Manage prioritises and acts on risks throughout the AI lifecycle. Manage activities include allocating resources to address identified risks, implementing controls, planning for incident response, and making decisions about whether to deploy, modify, or retire an AI system. Management of AI risk is an ongoing process, not a one-time gate.

Trustworthy AI Characteristics: The NIST AI RMF defines a set of characteristics that contribute to trustworthy AI. These include: validity and reliability — the system performs as intended under expected and unexpected conditions; safety — the system does not cause undue harm; security and resilience — the system resists adversarial attacks and recovers from failures; accountability and transparency — the system's behaviour and decisions can be explained and attributed; explainability and interpretability — outputs can be understood by relevant stakeholders; privacy enhancement — personal data is handled appropriately; and fairness with bias managed — the system does not produce discriminatory outcomes across groups. These characteristics involve trade-offs and should be balanced according to the deployment context rather than maximised in isolation. For example, a highly explainable model may sacrifice some accuracy; a highly privacy-preserving system may reduce utility. The AI RMF does not prescribe how to balance these properties but provides a vocabulary and structure for making those trade-offs explicit and documented.

title: EU Artificial Intelligence Act — Risk Classification and Requirements for High-Risk AI Systems
source: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council — Artificial Intelligence Act (EU AI Act), June 2024
content:
The EU Artificial Intelligence Act (EU AI Act) is the world's first comprehensive legal framework for artificial intelligence, establishing obligations based on a tiered risk classification system. It applies to providers, deployers, importers, and distributors of AI systems in the EU market.

Risk Classification — Four Tiers: The EU AI Act classifies AI systems into four risk tiers. (1) Unacceptable risk: AI systems whose risks are considered unacceptable are prohibited. Prohibited applications include social scoring by public authorities, real-time biometric surveillance in public spaces (with narrow exceptions), AI that exploits psychological vulnerabilities, and systems that manipulate individuals against their interests. (2) High risk: AI systems that pose significant risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights are subject to strict requirements before they can be placed on the market. High-risk systems include those used in biometric identification, critical infrastructure, education, employment, access to essential services, law enforcement, migration, and administration of justice. (3) Limited risk: AI systems with specific transparency obligations — for example, chatbots must disclose that the user is interacting with an AI. (4) Minimal risk: The vast majority of AI applications face no specific obligations, though providers are encouraged to follow voluntary codes of conduct.

General-Purpose AI Models (GPAI): The EU AI Act introduces obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models — large-scale foundation models such as large language models. GPAI providers must produce and maintain technical documentation, comply with EU copyright law, and publish summaries of training data. GPAI models posing systemic risk (above a defined compute threshold) face additional obligations including adversarial testing, incident reporting, and cybersecurity measures.

Requirements for High-Risk AI Systems: Before a high-risk AI system can be deployed, providers must implement: (a) a risk management system that operates throughout the lifecycle and identifies, analyses, and mitigates reasonably foreseeable risks; (b) data governance practices ensuring training, validation, and test data are relevant, representative, and free from errors; (c) technical documentation sufficient for conformity assessment; (d) record-keeping and logging to support post-market monitoring; (e) transparency obligations enabling deployers and users to understand how the system works and interpret its outputs; (f) human oversight measures enabling effective oversight by natural persons during operation, including the ability to override, interrupt, or correct outputs; and (g) accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity requirements. High-risk systems must undergo a conformity assessment and be registered in an EU database before market entry.

Human Oversight: The requirement for human oversight is a central feature of the high-risk tier. Deployers must assign oversight to natural persons who have the competence to understand the system's outputs, the authority to act on them, and the ability to override automated decisions. The degree of oversight must be proportionate to the risk and the reversibility of the decision. For AI systems used in law enforcement, migration, or judicial contexts, stronger oversight requirements apply.

title: ISO/IEC 42001 — Artificial Intelligence Management System Requirements
source: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 — Information technology — Artificial intelligence — Management system, International Organization for Standardization, December 2023
content:
ISO/IEC 42001:2023 is the first international standard specifying requirements for an Artificial Intelligence Management System (AIMS). It gives organisations a certifiable, process-based framework for the responsible development, provision, and use of AI systems. The standard follows the management system structure (High Level Structure) used by ISO 27001 for information security and ISO 9001 for quality management, making it integrable into existing management system frameworks.

Scope and Purpose: ISO/IEC 42001 applies to any organisation that develops, provides, or uses AI systems. It is technology-neutral — it covers narrow AI, machine learning, and foundation models equally. The standard is intended to help organisations demonstrate responsible AI governance to customers, regulators, and other stakeholders, and to provide a basis for third-party certification.

Management System Requirements: Like ISO 27001, ISO/IEC 42001 requires organisations to: (a) establish the context of the organisation and the scope of the AIMS; (b) demonstrate leadership commitment to responsible AI, including a policy for AI governance; (c) plan for risks and opportunities associated with AI systems, including an AI impact assessment; (d) provide adequate resources, including competent personnel and appropriate infrastructure; (e) implement operational controls covering the AI lifecycle from development through deployment and retirement; (f) evaluate performance through internal audit and management review; (g) continually improve the AIMS based on evaluation findings.

AI Impact Assessment: A distinctive feature of ISO/IEC 42001 is the requirement for an AI impact assessment — a structured process to identify and evaluate the potential impacts of an AI system on individuals, groups, and society. Impact assessment must be proportionate to the risk profile of the system and must consider fairness, privacy, safety, security, transparency, and accountability dimensions.

Controls Annex: The standard includes an Annex A with a set of AI-specific controls covering: responsible AI policy and governance; AI lifecycle management; data for AI (quality, provenance, and governance); AI system safety, security, and robustness; transparency and explainability; human oversight and intervention; and management of third-party AI components. Organisations select controls appropriate to their risk profile and document the rationale for any controls not applied.

Relationship to Other Frameworks: ISO/IEC 42001 is designed to complement rather than replace sector-specific requirements. For financial institutions, it can be implemented alongside OSFI Guideline E-23 (model risk management) and NIST AI RMF. The standard's management-system approach provides the organisational governance layer; sector-specific frameworks provide the technical and risk-specific requirements.

title: AI Governance Principles — Human Oversight, Audit Logging, Model Validation, and Retrieval-Augmented Generation
source: CloudKraft AI Governance Reference — Synthesised from NIST AI RMF, OSFI E-23, EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001, and published AI governance literature
content:
This document summarises four foundational AI governance concepts referenced across major regulatory frameworks: human-in-the-loop oversight, immutable audit logging, model validation, and retrieval-augmented generation.

Human-in-the-Loop Oversight: Human-in-the-loop (HITL) oversight inserts a point of human review before a consequential AI output is acted upon. This supports accountability, allows errors and unsafe outputs to be caught before they cause harm, and satisfies governance expectations for human oversight found across frameworks such as the EU AI Act (Article 14 — human oversight measures for high-risk AI) and OSFI E-23 (human review requirements proportionate to materiality and reversibility). The degree of oversight should be proportional to the impact and reversibility of the decision — automated decisions with irreversible or high-stakes consequences require stronger oversight than low-stakes, reversible ones. Human oversight provides a control point where consequential AI outputs are reviewed before action is taken. Required oversight should scale with the impact and reversibility of the decision, and is reflected in multiple regulatory frameworks including the EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF (GOVERN 1.2, MANAGE 2.1), and OSFI E-23.

Immutable Audit Logging: An immutable, append-only audit log records system actions, inputs, outputs, and human approvals in a way that cannot be silently altered or deleted. This provides verifiable evidence for regulators and auditors, supports incident investigation, and enables reconstruction of how a given decision was produced. Tamper-resistance is what distinguishes an audit record from ordinary application logging. An append-only audit log captures actions, inputs, outputs, and approvals as tamper-resistant evidence. Immutability is typically enforced through database-level constraints — for example, a BEFORE DELETE trigger that prevents any deletion from the audit table. Immutability supports regulatory accountability, incident investigation, and reconstruction of decisions. NIST AI RMF maps this to GOVERN 1.4 and MAP 4.1 (accountability and traceability); OSFI E-23 requires traceability and ongoing accountability in the Monitoring phase of the model lifecycle.

Model Validation: Validation is an independent assessment of whether a model is conceptually sound and fit for its intended use before deployment. It typically reviews the model's design and assumptions, tests performance and accuracy against held-out or benchmark data, checks data quality and representativeness, assesses limitations and potential failure modes, and confirms that controls and documentation are adequate. Validation independently assesses conceptual soundness and fitness for intended use prior to deployment. It reviews assumptions, tests performance and data quality, identifies limitations, and confirms adequate controls and documentation. Validation should be performed with appropriate independence from development — validators must have no stake in model outcomes and sufficient expertise in the model types they assess. OSFI E-23 requires independent validation before deployment of any material model and at minimum annually thereafter for high-risk models.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG): Retrieval-augmented generation is a pattern where a language model's response is grounded in documents retrieved from an external knowledge source at query time, rather than relying only on the model's internal parameters. The retrieval step selects the most relevant documents from a vector store or search index; the generation step produces a response conditioned on those retrieved documents. RAG grounds model output in externally retrieved documents at query time rather than relying solely on model parameters. Grounding supports citation — responses can reference specific source material — and reduces hallucination by constraining the model to information present in the knowledge base. It also allows the knowledge source to be governed and updated independently of the model, which is significant for regulated use cases where the currency and provenance of information matters. From a governance perspective, RAG makes AI system outputs more auditable: citations allow reviewers to verify that claims are supported by authoritative source material.
